items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Once again I’m tormented
by vague misgivings...
Shpak’s missing a tape recorder,
the ambassador’s missing a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core comedic effect is mock-heroic/highbrow phrasing (“терзают смутные сомнения”) applied to petty theft logic, plus the elliptical parallelism “У Шпака — …, у посла — …” meaning they each LOST those items (not possess them). The actual translation preserves the high-register ‘tormented by vague misgivings’ and correctly renders the loss with ‘missing’. Parallel structure is kept well enough for the callback.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—very pleased.
Tsar—very pleased. Tsar.
Very pleased. Hello, Tsar.

Tsar—very pleased.
Hello, Tsar! Very pleased!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The humor is the banal, repetitive Soviet-style politeness and self-introduction (“царь” as if it were a job title/name tag), made more absurd by drunkenness and the royal context. The translation keeps the repetition and awkward social-cliché rhythm. It slightly shifts from “I’m the Tsar” to direct address “Hello, Tsar,” which weakens the ‘introducing himself as Tsar’ gag, but the repeated “Tsar—very pleased” still signals the intended effect.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical in meaning and function; preserves the sudden authoritarian royal persona and the comic escalation. No loss of tone.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman—I'm in mourning.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: The line’s humor relies on archaic/high-register melancholy in a mundane Soviet setting, plus the insulting mismatch of calling her “старушка.” “Leave me, old woman” is good. However, “I’m in mourning” implies bereavement/ritual mourning rather than general sorrow/печаль, shifting the meaning and slightly distorting the regal-melancholy register.

items.translation:
Bricked in!
Bricked in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Captures the core misunderstanding (being ‘walled in’) and the accusatory ‘demons’ as agent. “Bricked in” is a workable idiom for the situation and keeps the punchy repetition. Slight loss of the drawn-out lamenting ellipsis, but not essential.

items.translation: So that’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Meaning is correct and the religious framing is preserved (important given the Soviet-atheist comedic edge). The reference’s “Behold the power…” is more mock-biblical/solemn; the actual version is slightly more conversational (“So that’s what…”), reducing the grandiosity a bit but still works.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Functionally conveys the command to start dancing, but loses the showman/MC catchphrase feel of the Russian (a set formula akin to ‘Everyone dance!’ as an announcement). “Everybody dance!” is understandable but less idiomatic as an emcee line in English than something like “Everybody’s dancing!” / “Everyone, dance!” / “All dance!”; it also slightly flattens the comic ‘event-host’ vibe noted in the reference.

items.translation: I picked a good time to drop by!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the cheerful self-satisfaction and opportunistic timing. “Drop by” fits the casual criminal’s tone and keeps the comedic understatement. No meaningful loss.

items.original: Икра черная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Overseas “caviar”—eggplant spread!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Key creative constraint: avoid calling eggplant spread ‘caviar’ straightforwardly, because the joke is the Soviet product being absurdly presented as exotic delicacy; the reference explicitly warns against ‘caviar’ for clarity. The actual translation uses scare quotes around “caviar” and then clarifies “eggplant spread,” which largely solves the confusion while still echoing the original phrasing. This is a good compromise; minor risk of still implying it is a kind of caviar, but the quotes and ‘spread’ mitigate it.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
it’s all gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The comedic mechanism is the escalating, status-item inventory with obsessive counting, plus the slightly bungled delivery. The translation preserves the list, the repetition of “three,” and the ‘domestic’ qualifier. “Suede jacket—three” is clear and keeps the punch. No major issues.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And you’ll be cured!

And you will be cured too!

And I will be cured too!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Meaning is preserved (resigned ‘we’re all going to the psych ward’ punchline). However, shifting from impersonal “they’ll cure” (evoking institutions/psychiatry) to passive/attributeless “you’ll be cured” weakens the Soviet-era subtext and the darkly comic reference to psychiatric ‘treatment.’ Also the ellipsis-driven, sing-song cadence is reduced by exclamation marks and more assertive punctuation.

summary.interpretation: 2.09 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.42 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
